analogy: Think of "India: Location" through the chapter's own opening images: INTRODUCTION This unit deals with • Location — space relations and India’s place in the world UNIT I 2026-27 You have already seen the map of India in the previous classes. Now you closely examine the map of India (Figure 1.1). Mark the southernmost and... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: INTRODUCTION, 1 Statute mile = about 1.6 km (1.584 km), 1 Nautical mile = about 1.8 km (1.852 km), 3 INDIA – LOCATION. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
India: Location is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that iNTRODUCTION This unit deals with • Location — space relations and India’s place in the world UNIT I 2026-27

The text explains that you have already seen the map of India in the previous classes.

The text explains that now you closely examine the map of India (Figure 1.1).

The text explains that mark the southernmost and northernmost latitudes and the easternmost and westernmost longitudes.

The text explains that the mainland of India, extends from Kashmir in the north to Kanniyakumari in the south and Arunachal Pradesh in the east to Gujarat in the west.

The text explains that india’s territorial limit further extends towards the sea upto 12 nautical miles (about 21.9 km) from the coast.

The text explains that statute mile = 63,360 inches Nautical mile = 72,960 inches 1 Statute mile = about 1.6 km (1.584 km) 1 Nautical mile = about 1.8 km (1.852 km) Our southern boundary extends upto 6°45' N latitude in the Bay of Bengal.

The text explains that let us try to analyse the implications of having such a vast longitudinal and latitudinal extent.

The text explains that if you work out the latitudinal and longitudinal extent of India, they are roughly about 30 degrees, whereas the actual distance measured from north to south extremity is 3,214 km, and that from east to west is only 2,933 km.

The text explains that consult Chapter 3 on the topic Latitude, Longitude and Time in the book Practical Work in Geography – Part I (NCERT, 2006) to find out.

The text explains that this difference is based on the fact that the distance between two longitudes decreases towards the poles whereas the distance between two latitudes remains the same everywhere.

The text explains that from the values of latitude, it is understood that the southern part of the country lies within the tropics and the northern part lies in the sub-tropical zone or the warm temperate zone.

The text explains that this location is responsible for large variations in land forms, climate, soil types and natural vegetation in the country.

The text explains that now, let us observe the longitudinal extent and its implications on the Indian people.

The text explains that from the values of longitude, it is quite discernible that there is a variation of nearly 30 degrees, which causes a time difference of nearly two hours between the easternmost and the westernmost parts of our country.

The text explains that you are familiar with the concept of Indian Standard Time (IST).

The text explains that thus India, as a country, is a physically diverse land providing occurrence of varied resources.

The text explains that school Bhuvan NCER T is a portal providing map-based learning to bring awareness among the students about country’s natural resources, environment and their role in sustainable development.

The text explains that choose the right answer from the four alternatives given below.

The text explains that (vi) How do you explain the variation in the area and population of NCT Delhi and the Andaman and Nicobar Islands?

The text explains that (iii) How is the latitudinal spread of India advantageous to her?

The text explains that (iv) While the sun rises earlier in the east, say Nagaland and also sets earlier, how do the watches at Kohima and New Delhi show the same time?

The text explains that (i) On a graph paper, plot the number of districts in Madhya Pradesh, Karnataka, Meghalaya, Goa, Kerala, Haryana.

The text explains that do the number of districts have some relationship with the area of the state?

The text explains that (ii) Which state amongst Uttar Pradesh, West Bengal, Gujarat, Arunachal Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, Tripura and Rajasthan is the most thickly populated and which one is the least densely populated?

The text explains that (iii) Identify the states with coastal boundaries.

The text explains that (iv) Arrange the states from west to east which have only land boundary.

The text explains that (v) List the Union Territories which have coastal location.

The text explains that (vii) On a graph paper, draw a bar diagram to show the area and population of all the Union Territories.

Source: NCERT Class 11 India Physical Environment, Chapter 1: India: Location

prelims_traps: Do not treat INTRODUCTION and 1 Statute mile = about 1.6 km (1.584 km) as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to India: Location; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
